text:
There it is again, fifty-one hours and then the gentle treachery of Sunday evening, sliding into the gloom of Monday's threshold. The weekend, much hyped, much anticipated, arrives like a houseguest who has barely set down his bags before announcing he must catch his train. One star, possibly two, withheld on principle.

Consider the architecture of the thing. You spend Friday recovering from the week that produced it, a kind of decompression no one warned you would devour an entire evening. Saturday, the supposed jewel, is squandered on errands the workweek made impossible: the groceries, the laundry, the small civic obligations of being a person. And Sunday, poor Sunday, is already haunted by its successor, a day lived under the long shadow of the alarm clock not yet set but spiritually ringing.

The marketing promised leisure. The product delivers logistics. One imagines lodging a complaint, except the customer service line is open only Monday through Friday, nine to five, precisely the window in which one has no need to call.

What galls most is the asymmetry. Five days of obligation, two of reprieve, and even those two are not truly yours but merely loaned at punishing interest. Time, it turns out, is the one luxury good sold exclusively in insufficient quantities, and the weekend is its sample size, its teasing trailer, its appetizer served on a plate too small to constitute a meal.

And yet, reader, I confess I would not surrender it. A flawed thing, briefly held, beats no thing at all. Two stars, rounded up to three out of sheer sentimental weakness, with the lingering wish, eternal and unmet, that the management might simply make it longer.
